text: Today I'll be showing my technique for a robotic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. This is a very common operation that I do in my practice.
The surgeon begins the operation by identifying the ligament of Treitz, a key anatomical landmark in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon measures and runs 100 centimeters of small bowel to establish the biliopancreatic limb during the robotic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon divides the omentum to ensure the Roux limb can reach the stomach without tension, preparing for the next step in the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon divides the small bowel, visually separating the biliopancreatic limb from the roux limb.
The surgeon operates a robotic stapler to divide the small bowel, separating the biliopancreatic limb from the roux limb.
The surgeon divides the small bowel mesentery to provide sufficient length and mobilization for the roux limb, following the division of the two ends of the small bowel.
The surgeon measures and runs another hundred centimeters of small bowel, designating it as the roux limb, which will be connected to the stomach.
The surgeon begins creating an anastomosis between the biliopancreatic limb and the roux limb, forming the common channel.
The surgeon checks the orientation of the roux limb to ensure it is not twisted and verifies that the mesentery is splayed open properly before proceeding with the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses scissors to create common enterotomies in both the biliopancreatic limb and the Roux limb, preparing these sections for anastomosis.
Both bowel segments are well vascularized, with minor bleeding observed at the site of division.
The surgeon introduces a stapler to perform the common jejunojejunostomy anastomosis, preparing to join the biliopancreatic limb and the Roux limb.
The surgeon completes the creation of the common channel by closing the anastomosis between the biliopancreatic limb and the Rue limb using a running absorbable V-lock suture in two layers.
The surgeon carefully closes the corners of the anastomosis using a running absorbable suture, ensuring a secure closure to prevent potential leaks.
The surgeon runs the suture forward after ensuring the corner is closed, progressing towards the superior corner of the anastomosis.
The surgeon begins lumbar suturing for a two-layer closure, following the introduction of a stapler for the common jejunojejunostomy anastomosis.
The surgeon begins closing the jejunojejunostomy defect using a running suture technique.
This is really important to do as this is a site of internal hernias. As the two balls have come together, there's a new space that can form underneath the anastomosis.
Small ball can transverse through there and become kinked, and it can lead to small ball strangulation, which it will require emergency surgery to fix.
The surgeon sutures the roux limb to the bottom of the stomach while adjusting the patient's position from flat to reverse Trendelenburg.
The surgeon secures the roux limb in place to facilitate pouch creation and ensure easy access for the upcoming gastrojejunal anastomosis.
The surgeon begins creating the gastric pouch by entering through the pars flaccida, initiating the first step of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the transection point on the stomach while preserving the left gastric artery. Using a vessel sealer, the lesser sac vessels are divided at the chosen transection point.
I typically try to do about five centimeters from the GE junction. In this patient, I left a little more, it looks like, but in general, five to six centimeters from the GE junction.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to perform horizontal stapling on the stomach, ensuring the stomach is clear before applying a 40mm section of the 60mm stapler load.

After completing the horizontal stapling, the surgeon inserts a 36 French Visigy tube from the esophagus into the gastric pouch. A robotic stapler is introduced for vertical stapling, with one to two firings snugly aligned against the Visigy tube to aid in pouch creation.

The surgeon positions the Rulim in opposition to the newly created gastric pouch. A stay stitch is placed at the apex of the anastomosis to relieve tension, allowing retraction of the Rulim and stomach for the gastrotomy creation.
The surgeon uses scissors to create a gastrotomy against the Visigy, followed by performing an aneurotomy.
The surgeon creates the gastrojunal anastomosis using a robotic stapler, inserting approximately thirty millimeters of the sixty millimeter stapler load to join the gastric pouch and the jejunum.

The surgeon passes a bougie through the newly created gastrojunal anastomosis to ensure patency and prevent narrowing during suturing. A V-lock suture is used for closure, similar to the technique used for the jejunostomy anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a two-layer closure of the anastomosis, ensuring the bottom corner is completely sealed to prevent potential leakage.

The surgeon reaches the apex of the common channel of the gastrojejunal anastomosis and begins lumbar suturing, taking large bites to ensure a secure closure. A bougie is visible, maintaining the patency of the anastomosis, which was stapled at approximately thirty millimeters.

The surgeon applies a VLOC suture to relieve tension at the apex of the gastrojejunal anastomosis. The stay suture is reinforced, followed by lumbar sutures across the anterior surface of the anastomosis.
The surgeon reinforces the gastrojejunal anastomosis using large suture bites, ensuring the patency of the anastomosis with a bougie in place.

The surgeon inserts a scope through the mouth, down the esophagus, into the pouch, and through the gastrojejunal anastomosis. The assistant dribbles water over the anastomosis while the surgeon blows air to check for bubbling, indicating leaks. The anastomosis is visibly patent with no bubbling observed, confirming a successful bypass.
Thank you for watching.
